ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for Chinese broccoli (kailan) as a raw vegetable. The prepared dish — baby kailan stir-fried with oyster sauce — is not individually represented; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers Chinese broccoli as a raw commodity. The prepared stir-fried dish with oyster sauce is not individually coded; closest_parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of baby kailan with oyster sauce is approximately 150 g, presented as a side dish plate of stir-fried or blanched baby Chinese broccoli dressed with oyster sauce and garlic.

nutrient_source.title: Kailan (Chinese Broccoli), cooked, stir-fried — adapted from USDA FoodData Central: Chinese broccoli, raw (FDC ID 169975) and oyster sauce (FDC ID 173590), with cooking-loss adjustments
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for baby kailan with oyster sauce exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD at time of processing. Values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for raw Chinese broccoli (FDC ID 169975) with standard oyster sauce contribution (FDC ID 173590) and applying a 10% cooking-loss factor for water-soluble nutrients. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of non-starchy vegetables — analogous estimate based on cooked Chinese broccoli and leafy green vegetable category data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for baby kailan with oyster sauce is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 32 is estimated by analogy with cooked non-starchy leafy green vegetables (broccoli GI ~10–35, mixed cooked greens ~25–35). A conservative mid-range value of 32 is used. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (32 × 5.2 × 1.50) / 100 = 2.5, classified as low. The oyster sauce contribution adds minimal carbohydrate (~1–2 g per serving) and does not materially alter the GI category.

hypertension.guidance: A 150 g serving contributes approximately 570 mg sodium, which represents about 25% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced oyster sauce or consume this dish in moderation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 32 and a glycemic load of 2.5 per serving, baby kailan with oyster sauce has minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose and is generally suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though sodium from oyster sauce should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary fibre, low saturated fat, and beneficial micronutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Baby kailan provides dietary fibre, vitamin C, folate, and potassium that support cardiovascular health; saturated fat is low at 0.5 g per 100 g, though the sodium content from oyster sauce warrants attention for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baby Kailan with Oyster Sauce (蚝油炒小芥兰) is a popular Chinese-Malaysian and Singaporean vegetable side dish made from baby kailan — the tender young shoots of Chinese broccoli (Brassica oleracea var. alboglabra) — stir-fried or briefly blanched and then dressed with oyster sauce, garlic, and a drizzle of sesame oil. The dish is characterised by its vibrant dark-green colour, slightly bitter and earthy flavour, crisp-tender texture, and the rich umami glaze of oyster sauce. Baby kailan is prized for its more delicate stems and leaves compared to mature kailan, making it a premium vegetable choice in Chinese restaurants across Malaysia and Singapore. Nutritionally, the dish is low in calories (approximately 62 kcal per 100 g), provides moderate plant protein (2.8 g), dietary fibre (2.0 g), and is rich in vitamin C, vitamin A, folate, calcium, and iron. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, contributed by oyster sauce, which can reach 380 mg per 100 g. The glycemic index is low (approximately 32), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. Clinically, this dish is appropriate for most dietary patterns but should be consumed in moderation by individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium content. It is not suitable for vegans or strict vegetarians due to oyster sauce, and contains potential allergens including molluscs, soy, and gluten from oyster sauce. The dish is commonly served as a side in Malaysian Chinese restaurants, zi char stalls, and Singaporean hawker centres.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for baby kailan with oyster sauce was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD at time of processing. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for raw Chinese broccoli and oyster sauce with cooking-loss adjustments. GI is estimated by analogy with cooked non-starchy leafy green vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 169975 for Chinese broccoli, FDC ID 173590 for oyster sauce) with a 10% cooking-loss adjustment; no exact HPB or MyFCD entry was available at time of processing. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for this plant-based dish with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central source data. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based vegetable dish with no animal-derived ingredients other than oyster sauce, which contributes negligible B12; confirmed zero per USDA source. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero for this cooked vegetable dish; confirmed zero per USDA source. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as the dish is plant-based; oyster sauce contributes negligible cholesterol; confirmed zero per USDA source. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (1.8), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.9), and all micronutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central and are -1 only where the source does not report the nutrient; all reported values are numeric. 7. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on cooked non-starchy leafy green vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement exists for this dish. 8. Halal status is uncertain as oyster sauce may contain non-halal additives depending on brand; is_halal set to false as a conservative default. 9. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Fish and seafood' to 'Vegetables' as baby kailan is a vegetable dish; the 'Oyster' in the dish name refers to oyster sauce, not oyster seafood. 10. Sentinel value documentation: no -1 sentinel values remain in per_100g; all fields are populated with numeric estimates or confirmed zero values from USDA FoodData Central.
